macro_segments.title: Summary of Previous Daf: Taste (Ta'am) versus Sixty (Shishim) as Standards for Determining Admixture

micro_segments.title: Overview of Gid Hanashe and the Principle of Ta'am Ke'Ikar (Taste as the Thing Itself)
Methods of Transferring Taste: Cooking, Roasting, Salting, and Pickling
Two Models for Determining Taste: Experiential Models (Onion) versus Quantitative Rules (Sixty)
Non-Jewish Taster and Integration of Multiple Approaches by Rava
Sephardic versus Ashkenazi Approaches to Taste and Shishim

macro_segments.title: Non-Kosher Eggs: Conditions Under Which Eggs Impart Taste

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Eggs and the Sixty Measure: Eggs Require Sixty and Do Not Count Toward the Sixty
Rav Idi bar Avin's Challenge: Eggs Have Hard Shells and Impart Minimal Taste
Resolution: Eggs with Developing Chicks Impart Taste; Eggs from Non-Kosher Species Do Not
Baraita on Mixing Kosher and Non-Kosher Eggs: Clarification that Non-Kosher Refers to Eggs Containing Chicks
Absorption of Taste by Eggs: Whether Hard Shells Prevent Absorption

macro_segments.title: The Case of Fat Falling into Meat: Measuring Sixty Based on Current Proportions Versus Original Proportions

micro_segments.title: Rav Assi's Attempt to Be Lenient: Measuring Based on What the Pot Absorbed Over Time
Rabbis' Objection: The Pot Absorbs Proportionally, So Current Proportions Indicate Original Proportions

macro_segments.title: Half a K'Zayit of Fat and the Question of Thirty Versus Sixty: Rabbinic Stringency with Measures

micro_segments.title: Mar bar Rav Ashi's Proposal: Thirty Halves Suffice Against a Half-Zayit
Rav Ashi's Rebuke: Do Not Be Lenient with Rabbinic Measures; Taste Is Often Less Than Sixty
Rabbi Yochanan's Position: Even Half a Shiur Is Biblically Prohibited (Chatzi Shiur Asur)

macro_segments.title: Sixty or Sixty-One for Eggs: Ambiguity in the Tradition and the Stringency Applied to Eggs

micro_segments.title: Tradition in the Name of Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi: Egg Forbidden at Sixty, Permitted at Sixty-One
Rav Zeira's Critique: The Ambiguity in the Tradition Regarding Whether Counting Includes or Excludes the Original Egg
Rav Yitzchak's Statement: Clarifying Sixty Plus the Egg (Total Sixty-One) as the Standard

macro_segments.title: Evolution of the Sixty Measure: From Uncertain Numbers in Tannaitic Sources Toward Crystallization of Sixty

micro_segments.title: Rabban Gamliel bar Rabbi: Father Did Not Accept Forty-Seven; How Can I Accept Forty-Five?
Rabbi Shimon bar Rabbi: Father Would Not Accept Forty-Five; How Can I Accept Forty-Three?
Alternative Reading (Rashi): These Authorities Were Lenient, Permitting Forty-Seven and Forty-Five Respectively
Development from Taste-Based Models to Universal Quantitative Standards
Rabbi Chiya's Question: Is There Even Thirty? (Exaggeration to Dismiss an Insufficient Case)

macro_segments.title: Learning Out Sixty from Scripture: All Forbidden Things Measured by Sixty (with Alternative Tradition of One Hundred)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chiya bar Abba in the Name of Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi: All Torah Prohibitions Measured by Sixty
Alternative Tradition: All Torah Prohibitions Measured by One Hundred

macro_segments.title: The Ram's Foreleg as a Biblical Model: Determining Whether Sixty or One Hundred Is the Correct Measure

micro_segments.title: The Case of the Ram Offered by a Nazir: The Foreleg (Zeroa) Reserved for the Kohen Cooked with the Rest of the Ram
Tannaitic Dispute: Whether the Foreleg Is Cooked Whole, Whether Separately or Together with the Ram
Two Interpretations of Proportions: Meat and Bones Together (Sixty) Versus Meat Alone (One Hundred)
Rashi's Question: Do Bones Absorb and Impart Taste? How Are Proportions Determined?

macro_segments.title: The Foreleg as an Exception or a General Rule: Can We Learn Out Sixty and One Hundred to All Forbidden Things?

micro_segments.title: Objection: The Foreleg Case Is Exceptional—It Is Permissible (Mutar) That Would Normally Be Forbidden
Abaye's First Answer: This Case Is an Exception According to Rabbi Yehuda, Who Normally Disallows Bitul by Min Bemino
Source from Yom Kippur: Blood of Ox and Goat Are Never Batel Even When Mixed, Supporting Rabbi Yehuda's Principle
Why Sixty and One Hundred Are Not Learned as Exceptions: We Learn Stringencies, Not Leniencies, from Exceptional Cases

macro_segments.title: Rava's Alternative Explanation: The Foreleg Case Teaches About Ta'am Ke'Ikar as a Prohibition by Kodashim Only

micro_segments.title: Rava: The Exception Means We Are Not Concerned with Ta'am Ke'Ikar in This Case (Suggesting It Applies Elsewhere)
Rashi's Implication: Ta'am Ke'Ikar Is a Problem Only by Kodashim; in Ordinary Cases, Sixty Is About Quantitative Insignificance
Tosafot's Disagreement: Ta'am Is a Biblical Concern Everywhere; Here All Tastes the Same, and Sixty Simply Ensures Bitul
Tosafot's Alternative to Rashi: Residual Taste (Tasam She'ein Meshtanin), Not Full Ta'am Ke'Ikar, Is What We Ignore Here
Baraita on Chatat: Anything Touching the Flesh of a Chatat Absorbs Its Status, Supporting the Reality of Taste Transfer
Conclusion: The Foreleg Case Is Extraordinary; We Learn Sixty and One Hundred as a Stringency, Not a Leniency
